text: The surgeon uses a five-port laparoscopic approach to commence dissection at the left of the duodenal jejunal flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is lifted, and dissection occurs posteriorly in the avascular plane. The redundant sigmoid colon is mobilized laterally, with the lateral peritoneal fold incised, separating it from the ureter and gonadal vessels. The sigmoid colon is retracted anteriorly, and the medial peritoneal fold is incised near the right ureter. A medial to lateral dissection is performed in the avascular embryonic plane, with care taken to preserve autonomic nerves.

The surgeon continues lateral dissection while carefully preserving the ureter and gonadal vessels. After achieving lymph nodal clearance, the inferior mesenteric artery is clipped and divided at its root. Posterior dissection proceeds in the extra mesorectal avascular plane, aided by adequate traction. The dissection extends to the pelvic floor, and the lateral peritoneal incision in the pelvis is extended to expose the left iliac vessels.

The surgeon identifies the obliterated umbilical artery arising from the internal iliac artery. The left obliterated umbilical artery is ligated and divided. The internal iliac artery is dissected distally, with the middle rectal artery and inferior vesicle artery being identified, ligated, and divided.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal fold cut anteriorly to identify and clip the left vas. The right peritoneal cut is extended inferiorly, exposing the right iliac vessels, and the superior vesicle artery, obliterated umbilical artery, middle rectal artery, inferior vesicle artery, and vas on the right side are clipped and divided. The bilateral peritoneal cuts are joined anteriorly, and dissection begins in the prevesical space. The urachus is divided, and dissection continues inferiorly to separate the bladder from the anterior abdominal wall up to the pubic arch. Lateral bladder attachments are divided using a harmonic scalpel, exposing the levators and endopelvic fascia on both sides.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the dorsal venous complex, exposing the prostatic capsule. The dorsal venous complex is fully exposed, clipped, sealed, and divided using a harmonic energy device.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the periurethral tissue and transect the urethra. The right ureter is clipped and divided at the uretero-vesical junction, then released from its retroperitoneal attachments. The left ureter undergoes the same procedure, being divided at the uretero-vesical junction and released from its retroperitoneal attachments.

The surgeon frees the left ureter to facilitate uretero-conduit anastomosis. The mesocolon is divided, and the distal descending colon is transected using a linear endoscopic stapler, completing the laparoscopic phase. The specimen is delivered perineally after perineal dissection. A six-centimeter lower midline laparotomy is performed, and an ileal conduit is created extracorporeally. Both the permanent colostomy and the conduit stoma are fashioned, and the midline incision is closed.
